ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class (FOODON_03302051); no specific vegetarian-filled steamed bun subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers steamed bread and buns as a category; no vegetarian-filled variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for steamed vegetarian buns. SNOMED CT does not have sufficient granularity for this prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 3 steamed vegetarian buns, approximately 60 g each, totalling 180 g, as served at Din Tai Fung and similar Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed Vegetable Bun (Bao) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo steamed bun category data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Din Tai Fung steamed vegetarian buns exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for steamed Chinese vegetable buns (wheat dough, vegetable and tofu filling) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo steamed bun category entries. The filling composition (nai bai, mushrooms, vermicelli, beancurd) is consistent with standard vegetarian bao nutritional profiles. Per-100g values reflect a blended dough-to-filling ratio of approximately 55:45 by weight, consistent with published steamed bun literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of steamed wheat buns and analogous filled steamed buns — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for steamed vegetarian buns (素菜包) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated from the closest analogous food: steamed wheat buns (mantou-style, plain) which have a reported GI of approximately 44–46 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The vegetable and tofu filling lowers the effective GI relative to plain steamed buns due to added dietary fibre and protein. GL per serving calculated as: (45 × 67.5 g available carbohydrate per 180 g serving) / 100 = 30.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~558 mg per 3-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 buns per meal, as a 3-piece serving contributes approximately 558 mg sodium (about 24% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and should avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or chilli.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low-to-moderate GI (estimated GI 45); 3-piece serving provides approximately 67.5 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~30
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat a 3-piece serving as a full carbohydrate portion for a meal, monitor post-prandial blood glucose response, and pair with non-starchy vegetables or a protein source to blunt the glycemic response; portion reduction to 1–2 buns is advisable for those with tighter glycemic targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.5 g per 100 g), no cholesterol from animal sources, plant-based filling with dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed vegetarian buns are a relatively heart-friendly choice due to their low saturated fat content and plant-based filling; however, sodium intake should be monitored and the buns should be consumed as part of a balanced meal rather than as a standalone high-carbohydrate snack.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Vegetarian Buns (素菜包, sù cài bāo) are a classic Chinese dim sum item consisting of a soft, fluffy, leavened wheat-flour dough wrapper that is filled with a savoury mixture of vegetables and plant-based proteins, then steamed until fully cooked. The Din Tai Fung version, served in Singapore, uses premium flour air-flown from Taiwan and a filling of nai bai (baby pak choy), shiitake mushrooms, glass vermicelli, and firm beancurd (tofu) cubes, seasoned with sesame oil and soy sauce. The bun skin is pillowy, slightly sweet, and brilliantly white, while the filling is moist, umami-rich, and lightly seasoned. Each bun weighs approximately 60 g, and a standard serving is 3 pieces (180 g total). Nutritionally, the buns are moderate in energy (~198 kcal per 100 g), relatively low in fat (~2.8 g per 100 g), and provide a meaningful carbohydrate load (~37.5 g per 100 g) with a low-to-moderate estimated glycemic index of 45. The vegetable and tofu filling contributes dietary fibre (~2.1 g per 100 g), plant protein (~6.2 g per 100 g), and micronutrients including calcium, iron, and vitamin C from the leafy greens. Sodium is moderate (~310 mg per 100 g) and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is vegetarian, dairy-free, and halal-suitable, making it broadly accessible. Clinically, the moderate glycemic load of a 3-piece serving (~30) means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should account for this as a full carbohydrate portion. The low saturated fat profile makes it a reasonable choice for cardiovascular health when consumed in appropriate portions. Steamed vegetarian buns are popular as a breakfast item, dim sum course, or light meal in Singapore, Malaysia, and across the Chinese diaspora.
common_user_queries: How many calories in steamed vegetarian buns?
Is vegetarian bao suitable for diabetics?
Is Din Tai Fung vegetarian bun halal?
How much protein in vegetable bao?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Din Tai Fung steamed vegetarian buns (素菜包) exists in any approved primary source (HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central steamed Chinese vegetable bun data and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo steamed bun category entries. The filling composition is consistent with the restaurant description. Confidence is medium because the source match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA FoodData Central steamed Chinese vegetable bun data and HPB Singapore Foodo steamed bun category data; no exact laboratory analysis for Din Tai Fung 素菜包 is available in any approved source. 2. Serving size of 180 g (3 × 60 g buns) is estimated from standard dim sum bun weights; Din Tai Fung does not publish per-piece weights in approved nutritional databases. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from steamed wheat bun data in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based filling with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as steamed vegetarian buns contain no significant vitamin D sources; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as the filling is entirely plant-based (no eggs, meat, or dairy); confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as the buns are steamed with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero by preparation method. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 0.9 estimated from sesame oil and soybean oil in tofu), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1 estimated similarly) are included as estimates. 9. The vegan flag is set to false because the dough may contain trace amounts of non-vegan processing aids depending on the specific flour used; the filling itself is vegan. 10. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
